human-readable stream with tool updates\nacpx codex 'review this PR'\n\n# json: NDJSON events, useful for automation\nacpx --format json codex exec 'review this PR' \\\n | jq -r 'select(.type==\"tool_call\") | [.status, .title] | @tsv'\n\n# json-strict: suppresses non-JSON stderr output (requires --format json)\nacpx --format json --json-strict codex exec 'review this PR'\n\n# quiet: final assistant text only\nacpx --format quiet codex 'give me a 3-line summary'\n\n# suppress read payloads while keeping the selected output format\nacpx --suppress-reads codex exec 'inspect the repo and report tool usage'\n```\n\n- `text`: human-readable stream with assistant text and tool updates\n- `json`: raw ACP NDJSON stream for automation\n- `quiet`: final assistant text new|ensure`, `status`) always include\n`acpxRecordId` and `acpxSessionId`. Session behavior\n\n- Prompt commands require an existing saved session record (created via `sessions new` or `sessions ensure`).\n- Prompts route by walking up from `cwd` (or `--cwd`) to the nearest git root (inclusive) and selecting the nearest active session matching `(agent command, dir, optional name)`.\n- If no git root is found, prompts only match an exact `cwd` session (no parent-directory walk).\n- `-s ` selects a parallel named session during that directory walk.\n- `sessions new [--name ]` creates a fresh session for that scope and soft-closes the prior one.\n- `sessions ensure [--name ]` is idempotent: it returns an existing scoped session or creates one when missing.\n- `sessions close [name]` soft-closes the session: queue owner/processes are terminated, record is kept with `closed: true`.\n- `sessions list` uses agent-side ACP `session/list` when available; use\n `--cursor`, `--filter-cwd`, or `--local` for pagination, cwd filtering, or\n saved-record inspection.\n- Auto-resume for cwd scope skips sessions marked closed.\n- Prompt submissions are queue-aware per session. If a prompt is already running, new prompts are queued and drained by the running `acpx` process.\n- Queue owners use an idle TTL (default 300s). `--ttl ` overrides it; `--ttl 0` keeps owners alive indefinitely.\n- `--no-wait` submits to that queue and returns immediately.\n- `cancel` sends cooperative `session/cancel` to the running queue owner process and returns success when no prompt is running (`nothing to cancel`).\n- `set-mode` and `set` route through queue-owner IPC when active, otherwise they reconnect directly to apply `session/set_mode` and `session/set_config_option`.\n- `` values for `set-mode` are adapter-defined; unsupported values are rejected by the adapter (commonly `Invalid params`).\n- `exec` is always one-shot and does not reuse saved sessions.\n- Session metadata is stored under `~/.acpx/sessions/`.\n- Each successful prompt appends lightweight turn history previews (`role`, `timestamp`, `textPreview`) to session metadata.\n- `Ctrl+C` during a running turn sends ACP `session/cancel` and waits briefly for `stopReason=cancelled` before force-killing if needed.\n- If a saved session pid is dead on the next prompt, `acpx` respawns the agent, attempts `session/resume` when advertised or `session/load` otherwise, and transparently falls back to `session/new` if reconnecting fails.\n\n## Full CLI reference\n\nSee [docs/CLI.md](docs/CLI.md).\n\n## License\n\nMIT\n","type":"text"}],"isError":false} <|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant ## Agent Client Protocol (ACP) **ACP** is a standardized protocol for communication between **code editors/IDEs** and **AI coding agents**. Think of it as the "Language Server Protocol (LSP)" for AI agents — it decouples editors from agents so either side can innovate independently. ### The Problem It Solves Before ACP, every editor had to build custom integrations for every agent, and every agent had to implement editor-specific APIs. This meant: - **Integration overhead** — each new agent-editor combo requires custom work - **Limited compatibility** — agents only work with a subset of editors - **Developer lock-in** — choosing an agent means accepting their available interfaces ### How It Works ACP supports two scenarios: | Type | Communication | |------|--------------| | **Local agents** | JSON-RPC over stdio (editor spawns agent as subprocess) | | **Remote agents** | HTTP or WebSocket (cloud-hosted agents) | The protocol reuses JSON representations from MCP where possible, but adds custom types for agentic UX elements like displaying diffs. Markdown is the default format for user-readable text. ### Ecosystem There's a growing registry of ACP-compatible tools: - **Agents**: Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, Copilot CLI, Pi, Qwen Code, and many more - **Implementations**: `acpx` (headless CLI client), `acp.el` (Emacs), `acp-ui` (cross-platform desktop/mobile/web) ### Key Takeaway ACP follows the same pattern LSP did for language servers: define a protocol once, then any editor can talk to any agent. This gives you the freedom to pick the best agent and the best editor independently.<|im_end|> <|im_start|>user ok so this is like subclassing MCP! this reminds me alot of Model Context Protocol (deja vu that I thought of that right before reading your comparison)!... so basically where MCP falls short for agent like task messaging and the things you need a UI to show for an Agent<|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant You're hitting on exactly the right distinction. Supported Behavior Notes\n\n#### Session cancel and controls\n\n- `acpx cancel` sends `session/cancel` through the queue path and keeps the session alive for follow-up prompts.\n- This is intentionally different from process-level SIGINT behavior, which can tear down the running process.\n- `session/set_mode` and `session/set_config_option` are exposed as `set-mode` and `set` commands and return agent-side validation errors when invalid.\n- Model config options are preferred; `session/set_model` remains available only when an adapter explicitly advertises legacy `models` metadata.\n\n#### Filesystem client methods\n\n- acpx handles `fs/read_text_file` and `fs/write_text_file` as client-authority operations.\n- Permission behavior follows selected mode (`--approve-all`, `--approve-reads`, `--deny-all`).\n- Path sandboxing is applied to keep file operations scoped to cwd by default.\n\n#### Terminal client methods\n\n- acpx handles full terminal lifecycle: create, output, wait_for_exit, kill, release.\n- The implementation includes process tracking, output buffering, and cleanup behavior for terminal IDs.\n\n#### Authentication\n\n- acpx handles `authenticate` when adapters request it.\n- This keeps compatibility with adapters that rely on ACP auth handshake rather than out-of-band environment setup.\n\n## Not Yet Supported\n\n| ACP Method | What it does | Spec status |\n| ------------------ | ----------------------------------- | ----------- |\n| `session/fork` | Branch a session into two | unstable |\n| `session/list` | List sessions server-side | unstable |\n| `session/resume` | Resume a paused session | unstable |\n| `$/cancel_request` | Cancel any pending JSON-RPC request | unstable |\n\n### Not Yet Supported Notes\n\n- `session/fork`: would allow branching one conversation into parallel alternatives.\n- `session/list`: would expose adapter-side session inventory in addition to acpx local store listing.\n- `session/resume`: distinct from `session/load`; expected to support resume semantics without replay-like behavior.\n- `$/cancel_request`: transport-level JSON-RPC cancellation beyond session-scoped cancel.\n\n## ACP-Adjacent Features Not Yet Supported\n\nThings acpx needs that aren't in the ACP spec:\n\n- [ ] **Permission policies** — Tool-kind/title policies now exist through `--permission-policy`; path/argument rules (`allow reads to src/`, `deny writes to .env`) are still not supported.\n- [ ] **Multi-agent orchestration** — Agent A prompts Agent B through acpx.\n Session bridging.\n- [ ] **Webhooks / callbacks** — Notify a URL when a prompt finishes. For CI/CD\n and automation pipelines.\n- [x] **Session export/import** — Move sessions between machines.\n- [ ] **Watch mode** — Re-run prompt on file changes.\n- [ ] **Cost/token tracking** — Surface usage stats when agents/ACP expose them.\n","type":"text"}],"isError":false} <|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant gh search repos agentclientprotocol/spec --limit 3 10000 <|im_end|> <|im_start|>user {"content":[{"type":"text","name":"EXIT_CODE","text":"0"}]} <|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant gh search repos agent-client-protocol --limit 5 10000 <|im_end|> <|im_start|>user {"content":[{"type":"text","name":"EXIT_CODE","text":"0"},{"type":"text","name":"STDOUT","text":"agentclientprotocol/agent-client-protocol\tA protocol for connecting any editor to any agent\tpublic\t2026-06-12T16:55:59Z\nopenclaw/acpx\tHeadless CLI client for stateful Agent Client Protocol (ACP) sessions\tpublic\t2026-06-12T11:57:22Z\nagentclientprotocol/registry\tRegistry of agents implementing the Agent Client Protocol (ACP)\tpublic\t2026-06-12T17:32:55Z\nRAIT-09/obsidian-agent-client\tBring AI agents into Obsidian via Agent Client Protocol (ACP), such as Claude Code, Codex and Gemini CLI.\tpublic\t2026-06-12T10:14:52Z\nagentclientprotocol/kotlin-sdk\tAgent Client Protocol Kotlin SDK\tpublic\t2026-06-11T01:01:43Z\n"}]} <|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant https://raw.githubusercontent.com/agentclientprotocol/agent-client-protocol/main/README.md <|im_end|> <|im_start|>user {"content":[{"text":"Content type text/plain; charset=utf-8 cannot be simplified to markdown, but here is the raw content:\nContents of https://raw.githubusercontent.com/agentclientprotocol/agent-client-protocol/main/README.md:\n\n \n\n\n# Agent Client Protocol\n\nThe Agent Client Protocol (ACP) standardizes communication between _code editors_ (interactive programs for viewing and editing source code) and _coding agents_ (programs that use generative AI to autonomously modify code).\n\nLearn more at [agentclientprotocol.com](https://agentclientprotocol.com/).\n\n## Versioning\n\nThe published crate and schema package versions describe the Rust crate and JSON Schema artifacts themselves. They follow the compatibility expectations of those artifacts: Rust APIs, generated schema structure, artifact layout, and other details that downstream SDKs or code generators may consume.\n\n**The current stable ACP protocol version is `1`.**\n\nACP wire compatibility is determined separately by the protocol version exchanged during `initialize` via `protocolVersion`. The `version` field in the versioned `schema/*/meta*.json` files also describes the ACP protocol version that the corresponding schema represents.\n\nThis means two versions of the JSON Schema artifacts can describe the same wire-compatible ACP protocol version while having different schema structure for SDK generators. For example, a release might change how definitions are organized, named, or emitted in the JSON Schema in a way that affects downstream code generation without changing the JSON messages exchanged by ACP clients and agents.\n\nConsumers should not infer wire compatibility from the crate or schema package version alone. Use the negotiated `protocolVersion` to determine the ACP wire protocol shape and breaking-compatibility level. Within a protocol version, use the exchanged capabilities to decide which optional ACP messages and features are supported. Use artifact versions to manage compatibility with this repository's Rust and schema outputs.\n\n## Integrations\n\n- [Schema](./schema/v1/schema.json)\n- [Agents](https://agentclientprotocol.com/overview/agents)\n- [Clients](https://agentclientprotocol.com/overview/clients)\n- Official Libraries\n - **Kotlin**: [`acp-kotlin`](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/kotlin-sdk) - Supports JVM, other targets are in progress, see [samples](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/kotlin-sdk/tree/master/samples/kotlin-acp-client-sample/src/main/kotlin/com/agentclientprotocol/samples)\n - **Java**: [`java-sdk`](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/java-sdk) - See [examples](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/java-sdk/tree/main/examples)\n - **Python**: [`python-sdk`](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/python-sdk) - See [examples](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/python-sdk/tree/main/examples)\n - **Rust**: [`agent-client-protocol`](https://crates.io/crates/agent-client-protocol) - See [examples/agent.rs](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/rust-sdk/blob/main/src/agent-client-protocol/examples/agent.rs) and [examples/client.rs](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/rust-sdk/blob/main/src/agent-client-protocol/examples/client.rs)\n - **TypeScript**: [`@agentclientprotocol/sdk`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@agentclientprotocol/sdk) - See [examples/](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/typescript-sdk/tree/main/src/examples)\n- [Community Libraries](https://agentclientprotocol.com/libraries/community)\n\n## Contributing\n\nACP is a protocol intended for broad adoption across the ecosystem; we follow a structured process to ensure changes are well-considered. The deja vu is well-founded — they share the same DNA (JSON-RPC, capability negotiation, content blocks) but solve different problems. Here's the breakdown: ## MCP vs ACP — What Each Owns | Dimension | **MCP** (Model Context Protocol) | **ACP** (Agent Client Protocol) | |-----------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------| | **Purpose** | Tool/resource discovery & invocation | Agent conversation lifecycle & UI rendering | | **Analogy** | REST API for agent capabilities | WebSocket-like stream for agent interaction | | **Core primitive** | `tools/list`, `resources/read` | `session/prompt`, `session/update` | ## Where MCP Falls Short for Agents ### 1. No session/conversation model MCP has no concept of a conversation with state across turns. ACP has `session/new`, `session/load`, `session/cancel`, `session/fork`. MCP tools are stateless fire-and-forget calls. ### 2. No streaming updates for progress MCP returns a single result from a tool call. ACP streams **updates** in real-time via `session/update` — the agent can report: - **Thinking** (reasoning steps) - **Tool call progress** (`pending` → `in_progress` → `completed`/`failed`) - **File locations** being worked on (for "follow-along" highlighting in the editor) ### 3. No diff content type MCP only has `text`, `image`, and `resource` content blocks. ACP adds a **`diff`** content type: ```json { "type": "diff", "path": "src/auth.ts", "oldText": "...", "newText": "..." } ``` This lets the editor show an inline diff view instead of just dumping text. ### 4. No terminal embedding ACP has a `terminal` content type that embeds a live terminal session by ID, so the editor can display running command output in-line with the conversation. ### 5. No permission request flow MCP just calls tools. ACP has `session/request_permission` where the agent asks the client to present approval options to the user (`allow_once`, `allow_always`, `reject_once`, `reject_always`). This is the "do you want me to run this command?" UX that MCP has no answer for. ### 6. No tool call lifecycle tracking In ACP, each tool call has an ID and progresses through statuses. The client can show a real-time progress panel. MCP just returns `{content: [...]}` — all context is lost after the fact. ## The Relationship ACP **reuses** MCP's `ContentBlock` types (text, image, resource) so agents can forward MCP tool outputs without transformation. But ACP wraps those content blocks in a richer interaction model: ``` MCP: agent → [call tool] → result text/image ACP: agent → [start session] → [stream thinking] → [show diff] → [request permission] → [run terminal] → [complete] ``` So yes — it's like MCP for the **interaction layer** that sits above MCP's **capability layer**. MCP tells you *what an agent can do*. ACP tells you *how to show the agent doing it* in your UI.<|im_end|> <|im_start|>user I would like to use it to build a standalone live trace viewer actually... has anyone built a viewer only tool for it so I could stream this from my harness to any tool that wants to listen<|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant Good instinct — there are actually a couple of existing viewers in the ecosystem. Let me dig into the most relevant ones: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MagnovaAI/acp-sdk-zig/main/README.md <|im_end|> <|im_start|>user {"content":[{"text":"Content type text/plain; charset=utf-8 cannot be simplified to markdown, but here is the raw content:\nContents of https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MagnovaAI/acp-sdk-zig/main/README.md:\n# Agent Client Protocol — Zig\n\n[![Zig 0.16.0](https://img.shields.io/badge/zig-0.16.0-f7a41d?logo=zig&logoColor=white)](https://ziglang.org/download/)\n[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-blue.svg)](./LICENSE)\n\nThe **Agent Client Protocol** (ACP) standardises communication between *code editors* — interactive programs for viewing and editing source code — and *coding agents* — programs that use generative AI to autonomously modify code.\n\nThis repository is a native **Zig** implementation. It gives you allocator-aware wire types, a transport-agnostic synchronous SDK, proxy orchestration, an interactive trace viewer, a reference agent binary, and a comptime-reflective schema generator.\n\n> **Status:** working core, public API stabilising toward `v0.1.0`. Wire format matches the canonical protocol; the public surface is unstable until tagged.\n\n---\n\n## Packages\n\n**Core SDK**\n\n- [`acp-schema`](./src/acp-schema/) — Wire-format types: methods, requests, responses, notifications, content variants, tool calls, errors. Forward-compatible `unknown` buckets on every public union so newer peers don't crash older clients.\n- [`acp`](./src/acp/) — Synchronous `Connection`, vtable-based `Transport`, comptime-typed `Dispatcher`, `Session` state machine, capability negotiation, fixed-capacity trace ring buffer.\n- [`acp-async`](./src/acp-async/) — Newline-delimited framer, `BufferPair` deterministic test transport, `FileTransport` over `std.Io.File`, subprocess `Child` spawn.\n\n**Proxy orchestration**\n\n- [`acp-conductor`](./src/acp-conductor/) — Compose N typed interceptors between client and agent ends with pass / short-circuit / drop semantics.\n- [`acp-trace-viewer`](./src/acp-trace-viewer/) — Interactive TUI for replaying captured traces.\n\n**Examples and testing**\n\n- [`acp-cookbook`](./src/acp-cookbook/) — Minimal runnable client and agent.\n- [`acp-test`](./src/acp-test/) — In-memory `PipePair` transport and end-to-end contract tests.\n- [`yopo`](./src/yopo/) — Reference agent binary that drives the full contract over an in-process pipe.\n\n**Tooling**\n\n- [`tools/gen_schema`](./tools/gen_schema/) — Comptime-reflective generator that emits a canonical JSON catalog of the public schema.\n\n---\n\n## Install\n\n```sh\nzig fetch --save git+https://github.com/MagnovaAI/acp-zig\n```\n\nIn your `build.zig`:\n\n```zig\nconst acp_dep = b.dependency(\"acp\", .{ .target = target, .optimize = optimize });\nexe.root_module.addImport(\"acp\", acp_dep.module(\"acp\"));\nexe.root_module.addImport(\"acp-schema\", acp_dep.module(\"acp-schema\"));\n```\n\n## Quick start\n\n### Drive a connection\n\n```zig\nconst std = @import(\"std\");\nconst acp = @import(\"acp\");\nconst schema = @import(\"acp-schema\");\n\npub fn main() !void {\n var gpa: std.heap.GeneralPurposeAllocator(.{}) = .init;\n defer _ = gpa.deinit();\n const allocator = gpa.allocator();\n\n const transport: acp.Transport = my_transport; // FileTransport, BufferPair, etc.\n var conn = acp.Connection.init(allocator, transport);\n\n const resp = try conn.request(\n schema.agent.InitializeResponse,\n schema.agent.method_initialize,\n .{ .protocolVersion = schema.ProtocolVersion.V1 },\n );\n defer resp.deinit();\n\n std.debug.print(\"peer speaks protocol v{d}\\n\", .{resp.value.protocolVersion.value});\n}\n```\n\n### Implement an agent\n\n```zig\nconst Agent = struct {\n pub const Params = schema.agent.InitializeRequest;\n pub const Result = schema.agent.InitializeResponse;\n pub fn handle(_: *@This(), _: std.mem.Allocator, params: Params) acp.AcpError!Result {\n return .{ .protocolVersion = params.protocolVersion };\n }\n};\n\nvar dispatcher = acp.Dispatcher.init(allocator);\ndefer dispatcher.deinit();\n\nvar agent: Agent = .{};\ntry dispatcher.registerRequest(schema.agent.method_initialize, Agent, &agent);\n\nvar conn = acp.Connection.init(allocator, transport);\nconn.setRequestHandler(dispatcher.requestHandler());\ntry conn.serve();\n```\n\nA full client+agent round-trip lives in [`src/acp-test/contract_handshake.zig`](./src/acp-test/contract_handshake.zig). The reference contract is in [`src/yopo/main.zig`](./src/yopo/main.zig).\n\n---\n\n## Build and test\n\n```sh\nzig version # must report 0.16.0\nzig build # build all libraries + binaries\nzig build test --summary all # 119 pass + 11 gated skips, zero leaks\nzig fmt --check src/ tools/ # formatting check\n\nzig build yopo # run the reference contract suite\nzig build gen-schema -- /tmp/schema.json # emit canonical schema catalog\nzig build trace-viewer # build the TUI viewer\n```\n\n### Unstable feature flags\n\nEach unstable method is gated behind its own build flag, off by default:\n\n```sh\nzig build test \\\n -Dunstable_logout=true \\\n -Dunstable_session_fork=true \\\n -Dunstable_session_resume=true \\\n -Dunstable_session_close=true \\\n -Dunstable_session_model=true\n```\n\nOther flags: `unstable_elicitation`, `unstable_nes`, `unstable_cancel_request`, `unstable_session_usage`, `unstable_session_additional_directories`, `unstable_llm_providers`, `unstable_message_id`, `unstable_boolean_config`, `unstable_auth_methods`.\n\n---\n\n## Design notes\n\n- **One error set across boundaries.** `AcpError` is the only error type returned across package boundaries — never `anyerror!T`. Failure modes are explicit.\n- **Allocator-aware everywhere.** Every owning type takes an allocator and exposes a matching `deinit`. No global allocator, no hidden heap.\n- **Forward-compatible unions.** Public tagged unions expose an `unknown: std.json.Value` variant so a peer running a newer revision never crashes an older client.\n- **Comptime-typed dispatch.** `Dispatcher` registers handlers by method name with concrete `Params` / `Result` types. The thunk parses, invokes, and re-marshals through a per-call arena.\n- **Per-frame tracing.** Attach a `TraceBuffer` to a `Connection` and every JSON-RPC frame is recorded with direction and a monotonic sequence number. Diagnostics never block the protocol path.\n\n## Layout\n\n```\nsrc/\n├── acp-schema/ # wire types + JSON codec\n├── acp/ # core SDK\n├── acp-async/ # framing + real-IO transports\n├── acp-conductor/ # proxy chain\n├── acp-test/ # in-memory transport + contract tests\n├── acp-trace-viewer/ # TUI viewer\n├── acp-cookbook/ # examples\n└── yopo/ # reference agent binary\ntools/\n└── gen_schema/ # canonical schema catalog generator\n```\n\n---\n\n## Contributing\n\n- **Bug reports:** open a [bug report issue](.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.md). Include `zig version`, OS / arch, and a minimal failing snippet.\n- **Pull requests:** the PR template lists the merge checklist. Each PR should keep `zig build test` green with zero leaks and `zig fmt --check src/ tools/` clean. Wire-format changes need an explicit note in the PR body.\n- **Larger proposals:** open a discussion first so we can align on the wire-format impact before any code lands.\n\nSee [CHANGELOG.md](./CHANGELOG.md) for what's in each release.\n\n## License\n\n[MIT](./LICENSE).\n","type":"text"}],"isError":false} <|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openclaw/acpx/main/examples/flows/replay-viewer/README.md <|im_end|> <|im_start|>user {"content":[{"text":"Content type text/plain; charset=utf-8 cannot be simplified to markdown, but here is the raw content:\nContents of https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openclaw/acpx/main/examples/flows/replay-viewer/README.md:\n# Flow Replay Viewer\n\nThis example app visualizes one saved flow run bundle at a time.\n\nFor the viewer semantics and UX/layout rules, see\n[docs/2026-03-27-flow-replay-viewer.md](../../../docs/2026-03-27-flow-replay-viewer.md).\nFor the live viewer transport and state-sync model, see\n[docs/2026-03-31-flow-replay-live-transport.md](../../../docs/2026-03-31-flow-replay-live-transport.md).\n\nIt is separate from the `acpx` CLI surface on purpose:\n\n- `acpx` writes replayable run bundles under `~/.acpx/flows/runs/`\n- this viewer reads those bundles and renders them in the browser\n\nThe viewer uses:\n\n- the run bundle manifest and projections\n- the trace log\n- bundled ACP session snapshots and raw session events\n- React Flow for the graph\n\n## Run it\n\nFrom the repo root:\n\n```bash\npnpm viewer\n```\n\nThen open [http://127.0.0.1:4173](http://127.0.0.1:4173).\n\nThe local viewer server always uses that fixed port. If another replay viewer is\nalready running there, the command reuses it instead of bouncing to a random\nnew port.\n\nUseful helper commands:\n\n```bash\npnpm viewer:open\npnpm viewer:status\npnpm viewer:stop\n```\n\nThe main path is the built-in **Recent runs** list sourced from:\n\n```text\n~/.acpx/flows/runs//\n```\n\nIf the viewer starts before any runs exist, it stays empty and waits for the\nfirst real run instead of falling back to a demo bundle. New runs appear in the\nleft sidebar automatically, and the first recent run opens on its own.\n\nWhen a recent run is still active, the sidebar and the selected run view update\nlive over the viewer WebSocket transport. The viewer keeps the accumulated\nhistory locally, so you can still rewind while new steps continue to arrive.\n\n## What it shows\n\n- the flow graph, with replay progression over the saved step attempts\n- selected step prompt, raw response, parsed output, and action receipts\n- the ACP conversation slice for the selected ACP step\n- the raw bundled ACP event slice for that step\n\nThe full flow definition remains the main graph. The run is shown as an overlay\non that graph rather than replacing it with an execution-only path.\n\n## Included sample\n\nThe bundled sample under `public/sample-run/` still exists for development and\ntest fixtures. It comes from a real run of `examples/flows/two-turn.flow.ts`\nagainst the repo's mock ACP agent, with the machine-specific paths sanitized\nfor readability.\n","type":"text"}],"isError":false} <|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nativai/acpx-ui/main/README.md <|im_end|> <|im_start|>user {"content":[{"text":"Failed to fetch https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nativai/acpx-ui/main/README.md - status code 404","type":"text"}],"isError":true} <|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant https://raw.githubusercontent.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/main/README.md <|im_end|> <|im_start|>user {"content":[{"text":"Content type text/plain; charset=utf-8 cannot be simplified to markdown, but here is the raw content:\nContents of https://raw.githubusercontent.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/main/README.md:\n# ACP UI\r\n\r\n\r\n\t\r\n\r\n\r\nA modern, cross-platform client for the [Agent Client Protocol (ACP)](https://agentclientprotocol.com/) on desktop, mobile, and the web. Connect to AI coding agents like GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Qwen Code, Codex CLI, OpenCode, OpenClaw, Kiro CLI, Hermes Agent, and any ACP-compatible agent from a unified interface.\r\n\r\n![ACP UI Screenshot](assets/screenshot.png)\r\n\r\n## 🌍 Try it in your browser\r\n\r\nNo install required — open **[https://acp-ui.github.io/](https://acp-ui.github.io/)** and connect to a remote ACP agent over WebSocket. The web build supports the same chat, sessions, permissions, and traffic-monitor features as the desktop and mobile apps; it only omits local stdio agents and host filesystem access (which require a local subprocess and aren't available in a browser tab).\r\n\r\n> Pages served over HTTPS can only open `wss://` URLs (browser mixed-content rule). For LAN `ws://` access, run the bundle locally (`npm run preview:web`) or use a `wss://` tunnel — see [Connecting from your phone or browser](#-connecting-from-your-phone-or-browser), the same setup works for the web build.\r\n\r\n## 📥 Installation\r\n\r\nDownload the latest release for your platform from [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases):\r\n\r\n| Platform | Download |\r\n|----------|----------|\r\n| **Web** | [https://acp-ui.github.io/](https://acp-ui.github.io/) — no install, opens in any modern browser |\r\n| **Windows** | [.msi installer](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) or [.exe (NSIS)](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) |\r\n| **macOS (Apple Silicon)** | [.dmg (ARM64)](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) |\r\n| **macOS (Intel)** | [.dmg (x64)](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) |\r\n| **Linux (x64)** | [.deb](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) or [.AppImage](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) or [.rpm](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) |\r\n| **Linux (ARM64)** | [.deb](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) or [.AppImage](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) or [.rpm](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) |\r\n| **Android** | [.apk](https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui/releases/latest) — sideload via \"Install unknown apps\" |\r\n| **iOS** | Build from source (see [Building for iOS](#building-for-ios)) — no prebuilt binary |\r\n\r\n> Mobile and web builds connect to remote agents over WebSocket. See [Connecting from your phone or browser](#-connecting-from-your-phone-or-browser) for how to expose a local agent so a phone or browser can reach it.\r\n\r\n### macOS first-launch note\r\n\r\nThe macOS `.dmg` builds are ad-hoc signed but **not** notarized (no paid Apple Developer account), so on first launch macOS shows a dialog like *\"Apple could not verify acp-ui is free of malware.\"* The app is not damaged — this is Gatekeeper's standard warning for un-notarized apps. Easiest fix, run once after installing or upgrading:\r\n\r\n```bash\r\nxattr -dr com.apple.quarantine /Applications/acp-ui.app\r\n```\r\n\r\nThen open the app normally. Alternatives if you'd rather not use the terminal:\r\n\r\n- **macOS 15 Sequoia and later** — open **System Settings → Privacy & Security**, scroll to the bottom, click **Open Anyway** next to the acp-ui entry, authenticate, then re-launch the app.\r\n- **macOS 14 Sonoma and earlier** — right-click (or Control-click) `acp-ui.app` in Finder → **Open** → click **Open** in the follow-up dialog.\r\n\r\n## ✨ Features\r\n\r\n- **Multi-Agent Support** — Connect to any ACP-compatible agent\r\n- **Remote agents over WebSocket** — Talk to agents on another machine via `ws://` / `wss://`\r\n- **Web app** — Run in any modern browser at [acp-ui.github.io](https://acp-ui.github.io/) without installing anything\r\n- **Mobile** — Android APK shipped on Releases; iOS via local Xcode build\r\n- **Foreground reconnect** — On mobile and the web, automatically reattaches to your session when the app/tab regains focus\r\n- **Idle keep-alive** — Sends a JSON-RPC `$/ping` heartbeat every 25 seconds so NAT/proxy idle timeouts don't drop your WebSocket\r\n- **Session Management** — Create, resume, and manage conversation sessions\r\n- **Rich Chat Interface** — Markdown rendering, syntax highlighting, tool call visualization\r\n- **Slash Commands** — Quick access to agent capabilities with `/command` syntax\r\n- **Permission Controls** — Approve or deny agent actions before execution\r\n- **Session Modes** — Switch between agent modes (ask, code, architect, etc.)\r\n- **Model Picker** — Select from available AI models (unstable API)\r\n- **Agent Thinking** — View the agent's reasoning process (collapsible)\r\n- **Environment Variables** — Configure per-agent environment variables (API keys, settings)\r\n- **Traffic Monitor** — Debug and inspect ACP protocol messages in real-time\r\n- **Hot-Reload Config** — Edit agent configurations without restarting (desktop)\r\n- **Cross-Platform** — Web (any modern browser), Windows, macOS (ARM/Intel), Linux (x64/ARM64), Android, iOS\r\n\r\n## 🎯 Default Agents\r\n\r\nACP UI comes pre-configured with these agents:\r\n\r\n| Agent | Package |\r\n|-------|---------|\r\n| [GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/github/copilot-language-server-release?tab=readme-ov-file#agent-client-protocol-acp-preview) | `@github/copilot-language-server` |\r\n| [Claude Code](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code) | `@agentclientprotocol/claude-agent-acp` |\r\n| [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) | `@google/gemini-cli` |\r\n| [Qwen Code](https://github.com/QwenLM/qwen-code) | `@qwen-code/qwen-code` |\r\n| [Auggie CLI](https://github.com/AugmentCode/auggie) | `@augmentcode/auggie` |\r\n| [Qoder CLI](https://github.com/qoder-ai/qodercli) | `@qoder-ai/qodercli` |\r\n| [Codex CLI](https://github.com/zed-industries/codex-acp) | `@zed-industries/codex-acp` |\r\n| [OpenCode](https://github.com/opencode-ai/opencode) | `opencode-ai` |\r\n| [OpenClaw](https://github.com/nicobailon/openclaw) | `openclaw` |\r\n| [Kiro CLI](https://github.com/aws/kiro) | `kiro-cli` |\r\n| [Hermes Agent](https://github.com/nichochar/hermes) | `hermes` |\r\n\r\n## 🛠️ Configuration\r\n\r\nAgent configurations are stored in:\r\n\r\n| Platform | Path |\r\n|----------|------|\r\n| Windows | `%APPDATA%\\acp-ui\\agents.json` |\r\n| macOS | `~/Library/Application Support/acp-ui/agents.json` |\r\n| Linux | `~/.config/acp-ui/agents.json` |\r\n| Android | `/data/data/formulahendry.acp_ui/files/agents.json` (managed via Settings UI) |\r\n| iOS | App sandbox — managed via Settings UI |\r\n| Web | Browser `localStorage` (key `acp-ui:agents`) — managed via Settings UI |\r\n\r\n> On mobile and the web the config file isn't user-accessible — add and edit agents through the in-app **Settings** dialog. Stdio agents are filtered out of the list since they can't run in a browser or on a phone. Web-app config is per-browser per-origin: it doesn't sync across machines, and clearing site data wipes it.\r\n\r\n### Local stdio agents (desktop)\r\n\r\n### Example Configuration\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"agents\": {\r\n \"GitHub Copilot\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"@github/copilot-language-server@latest\", \"--acp\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n },\r\n \"Claude Code\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"@agentclientprotocol/claude-agent-acp@latest\"],\r\n \"env\": {\r\n \"ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\": \"sk-ant-...\"\r\n }\r\n },\r\n \"Gemini CLI\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"@google/gemini-cli@latest\", \"--experimental-acp\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n },\r\n \"Qwen Code\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"@qwen-code/qwen-code@latest\", \"--acp\", \"--experimental-skills\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n },\r\n \"Auggie CLI\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"@augmentcode/auggie@latest\", \"--acp\"],\r\n \"env\": {\"AUGMENT_DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE\": \"1\"}\r\n },\r\n \"Qoder CLI\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"@qoder-ai/qodercli@latest\", \"--acp\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n },\r\n \"Codex CLI\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"@zed-industries/codex-acp@latest\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n },\r\n \"OpenCode\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"opencode-ai@latest\", \"acp\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n },\r\n \"OpenClaw\": {\r\n \"command\": \"npx\",\r\n \"args\": [\"openclaw\", \"acp\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n },\r\n \"Kiro CLI\": {\r\n \"command\": \"kiro-cli\",\r\n \"args\": [\"acp\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n },\r\n \"Hermes Agent\": {\r\n \"command\": \"hermes\",\r\n \"args\": [\"acp\"],\r\n \"env\": {}\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\n> **Note**: Environment variables are passed to the agent process on startup. Use these for API keys, custom settings, or overriding default behavior.\r\n\r\n### Remote agents over WebSocket\r\n\r\nFor agents running on another machine — or for connecting from a phone to an agent on your laptop — use the `websocket` transport instead of `command`:\r\n\r\n```json\r\n{\r\n \"agents\": {\r\n \"Copilot CLI (remote)\": {\r\n \"transport\": \"websocket\",\r\n \"url\": \"wss://acp.example.com/v1\",\r\n \"headers\": { \"Authorization\": \"Bearer YOUR_TOKEN\" }\r\n }\r\n }\r\n}\r\n```\r\n\r\nBoth `ws://` (cleartext, for LAN / Dev Tunnels) and `wss://` (TLS) are accepted. `Authorization: Bearer ` is propagated as a WebSocket subprotocol because browser/WebView WebSocket APIs cannot set custom HTTP headers.\r\n\r\n> **Note**: Filesystem RPCs (`fs/read_text_file`, `fs/write_text_file`) are only available on Tauri desktop (Windows, macOS, Linux). On mobile and web clients the capabilities are advertised as `false` and any incoming `fs/*` request from the agent is rejected with JSON-RPC `-32601 Method not found`. For remote agents the working directory path is interpreted on the **agent's host**, not on the client device.\r\n\r\n## 🌐 Connecting from your phone or browser\r\n\r\nThe mobile and web builds can only talk to remote agents (no subprocess in a phone or browser sandbox), so you need to expose a local stdio agent over a network endpoint. The recommended bridge is [`@rebornix/stdio-to-ws`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@rebornix/stdio-to-ws), which speaks ACP-over-WebSocket on one end and stdio on the other. The same setup works for the web build at [acp-ui.github.io](https://acp-ui.github.io/) — with one extra rule: the HTTPS page can only open `wss://` URLs (see [HTTPS pages must use `wss://`](#browser-only-https-pages-must-use-wss) below).\r\n\r\n### Same Wi-Fi (LAN)\r\n\r\nOn your computer:\r\n\r\n```sh\r\nnpx @rebornix/stdio-to-ws \"copilot --acp\" --port 3000 --persist --grace-period -1\r\n```\r\n\r\n- Allow inbound TCP 3000 in your OS firewall.\r\n - Windows (one-time, elevated PowerShell):\r\n ```powershell\r\n New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName \"stdio-to-ws\" -Direction Inbound -LocalPort 3000 -Protocol TCP -Action Allow -Profile Private\r\n ```\r\n- Find your computer's LAN IP (`ipconfig` on Windows, `ifconfig`/`ip a` on macOS / Linux).\r\n- In ACP UI on the phone, add a websocket agent with URL `ws://:3000/`.\r\n\r\n> **Android emulator** uses `ws://10.0.2.2:3000/`. **USB-tethered phone** can use `ws://localhost:3000/` after running `adb reverse tcp:3000 tcp:3000`.\r\n\r\n### From anywhere (Microsoft Dev Tunnels)\r\n\r\n`stdio-to-ws` exposes the agent on `localhost`; pair it with [Microsoft Dev Tunnels](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/developer/dev-tunnels/) to get a `wss://` URL reachable from the public internet.\r\n\r\n```sh\r\n# Terminal 1 — wrap the agent as a WebSocket on port 3000.\r\nnpx @rebornix/stdio-to-ws \"copilot --acp\" --port 3000 --persist --grace-period -1\r\n\r\n# Terminal 2 — expose port 3000 publicly. First-run prompts for login.\r\ndevtunnel host -p 3000\r\n```\r\n\r\n`devtunnel host` prints a URL like:\r\n\r\n```\r\nhttps://-3000..devtunnels.ms\r\n```\r\n\r\nUse the **`wss://...devtunnels.ms/`** form (replace `https` with `wss`) as the agent URL in ACP UI on the phone or in the [web app](https://acp-ui.github.io/).\r\n\r\n#### Stable URL across restarts\r\n\r\nThe ad-hoc URL changes every run. To get a reusable one:\r\n\r\n```sh\r\n# One-time setup\r\ndevtunnel user login\r\ndevtunnel create my-acp -a\r\ndevtunnel port create my-acp -p 3000 --protocol https\r\n\r\n# Every session afterwards\r\ndevtunnel host my-acp\r\n```\r\n\r\nReference: [Dev Tunnels CLI commands](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/developer/dev-tunnels/cli-commands).\r\n\r\n#### Browser-only: HTTPS pages must use `wss://`\r\n\r\nWhen you open ACP UI in a browser at [acp-ui.github.io](https://acp-ui.github.io/), the page is served over HTTPS, and the browser blocks plain `ws://` connections (mixed-content rule). Two options:\r\n\r\n- **Easy:** front your bridge with a `wss://` URL (Dev Tunnels above gives you one for free).\r\n- **LAN-only:** serve the bundle locally instead of the hosted site:\r\n\r\n ```sh\r\n git clone https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui.git\r\n cd acp-ui && npm install && npm run preview:web\r\n ```\r\n\r\n then open `http://localhost:4173/` and add a `ws://:3000/` agent as usual.\r\n\r\n#### Why `--persist --grace-period -1`?\r\n\r\nMobile OSes freeze backgrounded apps within seconds, dropping the WebSocket. `--persist` tells the bridge to keep the wrapped agent alive across disconnects, and `--grace-period -1` makes that timeout infinite. When ACP UI on the phone returns to the foreground, it transparently reattaches via `session/load` and your conversation resumes. Without persistence, you'd lose the running agent every time you switched apps.\r\n\r\n> **Tip**: a future `stdio-to-ws` release will integrate Dev Tunnels into the bridge itself (`--tunnel-name `, currently only on its `dev` branch). Once published you'll be able to collapse the two terminals into one.\r\n\r\n## 📖 Usage\r\n\r\n1. **Select an Agent** — Choose from the dropdown in the sidebar (☰ on mobile / narrow web).\r\n2. **Set Working Directory** — Pick a folder on desktop, or type an absolute path on mobile / web. The path is interpreted on the **agent's host**, not your device.\r\n3. **Create Session** — Tap **New Session** to start chatting.\r\n4. **Use Slash Commands** — Type `/` to see available commands.\r\n5. **Resume Sessions** — Tap a saved session in the sidebar to resume.\r\n\r\n## 🚀 Development\r\n\r\n### Prerequisites\r\n\r\n- [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) 18+\r\n- [Rust](https://rustup.rs/) 1.70+\r\n- Platform-specific build tools (see [Tauri Prerequisites](https://tauri.app/start/prerequisites/))\r\n\r\n### Setup\r\n\r\n```bash\r\n# Clone the repository\r\ngit clone https://github.com/formulahendry/acp-ui.git\r\ncd acp-ui\r\n\r\n# Install dependencies\r\nnpm install\r\n\r\n# Run in development mode (Tauri desktop)\r\nnpm run tauri dev\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Build for Production\r\n\r\n```bash\r\nnpm run tauri build\r\n```\r\n\r\n### Building / running the web app\r\n\r\nThe web app uses the same Vue 3 frontend, with the Tauri runtime swapped out for browser-native APIs (WebSocket, `localStorage`). It only supports remote agents over `ws://` / `wss://`.\r\n\r\n```sh\r\n# Dev server with HMR (default port 5173)\r\nnpm run dev:web\r\n\r\n# Production build → dist-web/\r\nnpm run build:web\r\n\r\n# Serve dist-web/ locally to verify the production bundle\r\nnpm run preview:web\r\n```\r\n\r\nThe live deployment at [acp-ui.github.io](https://acp-ui.github.io/) is published from `dist-web/` by [.github/workflows/deploy-web.yml](.github/workflows/deploy-web.yml) on every push to `main`.\r\n\r\n### Building for Android\r\n\r\nPrerequisites:\r\n\r\n- JDK 17 (Temurin recommended)\r\n- Android SDK platform 34, build-tools 34, NDK 26\r\n- Rust Android targets: `rustup target add aarch64-linux-android armv7-linux-androideabi i686-linux-android x86_64-linux-android`\r\n- Set `ANDROID_HOME` and `NDK_HOME` env vars\r\n\r\n```sh\r\n# `src-tauri/gen/android/` is gitignored; this regenerates it.\r\nnpm run tauri android init\r\n\r\n# Allow plain ws:// to LAN agents (the init template defaults this off via\r\n# a Gradle placeholder). Required for ACP UI's LAN-agent UX.\r\nsed -i 's|usesCleartextTraffic=\"\\${usesCleartextTraffic}\"|usesCleartextTraffic=\"true\"|' \\\r\n src-tauri/gen/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml\r\n\r\n# Debug-signed APK suitable for sideload.\r\nnpm run tauri android build -- --debug --apk\r\n# Output: src-tauri/gen/android/app/build/outputs/apk/universal/debug/app-universal-debug.apk\r\n```\r\n\r\nTo run on a device with hot-reload during development:\r\n\r\n```sh\r\nnpm run tauri android dev\r\n```\r\n\r\nIf the device can't reach the dev server, allow port `1420` through your firewall, or USB-tether the phone and run `adb reverse tcp:1420 tcp:1420` first.\r\n\r\n### Building for iOS\r\n\r\nPrerequisites:\r\n\r\n- macOS with Xcode 15+\r\n- An Apple Developer team for signing\r\n- Rust iOS targets: `rustup target add aarch64-apple-ios x86_64-apple-ios aarch64-apple-ios-sim`\r\n\r\n```sh\r\nnpm run tauri ios init\r\n```\r\n\r\nThen edit `src-tauri/gen/apple/_iOS/Info.plist` and add:\r\n\r\n```xml\r\nNSAppTransportSecurity\r\n\r\n NSAllowsArbitraryLoads\r\n\r\nNSLocalNetworkUsageDescription\r\nACP UI connects to ACP agents you configure, including agents on your local network.\r\n```\r\n\r\nBuild and install via Xcode (`.xcworkspace`), or run on a connected device:\r\n\r\n```sh\r\nnpm run tauri ios dev\r\n```\r\n\r\niOS doesn't ship a binary today because it requires per-developer signing and an Apple Developer Program membership.\r\n\r\n## 🔗 Links\r\n\r\n- [Agent Client Protocol](https://agentclientprotocol.com/)\r\n- [Tauri Documentation](https://tauri.app/)\r\n\r\n## 📄 License\r\n\r\nMIT License\r\n","type":"text"}],"isError":false} <|im_end|> <|im_start|>assistant Here's what exists today — and where the gap is: ## Existing Viewer-Adjacent Tools | Tool | What it does | Limitation | |------|-------------|------------| | **MagnovaAI/acp-sdk-zig** (`acp-trace-viewer`) | TUI replay of captured traces | Zig-only, tied to that SDK's ring buffer format | | **openclaw/acpx** (flow replay viewer) | Browser-based flow run visualization with React Flow | Only for acpx *flow* bundles, not raw ACP sessions | | **formulahendry/acp-ui** | Full chat client + "Traffic Monitor" for real-time protocol inspection | It's a full agent harness, not a standalone viewer | | **Hollyen/acpx-viewer** | Web UI for acpx session info | acpx-specific, not protocol-agnostic | ## You're describing **Stateful Inference with Context Caching**. Most people try to do this by treating every LLM call as stateless (re-sending the whole prompt). That's slow and expensive. But if you treat the summary as a persistent object and only push *deltas*, it becomes incredibly fast. Here is why your specific constraints make this work: ### 1. The "Small/Fast" Model You don't need GPT-4o to tell you an agent is stuck in a retry loop. A quantized **Qwen2.5-7B** or even **Llama-3.2-3B** is surprisingly good at pattern recognition on structured text. * **Latency:** ~50–100ms per update. * **Accuracy:** High enough for "Is it making progress?" or "Did it fail?" ### 2. Append-Only + KV Cache Reuse This is the magic. `llama.cpp` (and backends like vLLM) compute the **Key-Value (KV) Cache** for every token in the prompt. * **Prompt:** `[System] [History Summary] [New Frames]` * **Mechanism:** When you append new frames, the model *reuses* the KV cache for the `[System]` and `[History Summary]` tokens. It only computes the new tokens for `[New Frames]`. * **Result:** Inference time is proportional to the *new data*, not the total history. ### 3. Dedicated Cache Resources By running this on a separate process/thread with dedicated VRAM: * No UI jank. * The KV cache stays hot (loaded in GPU memory). * It effectively acts as a "real-time compression" of the session. --- ## The Implementation Pattern Here is how you structure the prompt to support **Delta Summarization**: ### The Prompt Strategy **Initial State:** ```text [SYSTEM] You are an ACP Trace Analyzer. Your job is to maintain a running summary of an AI agent's behavior. [SESSION START] Agent: codex Task: "Fix the flaky test in checkout" [EVENTS] - session/prompt received - tool_call: read_text_file (src/checkout.test.ts) -> completed - tool_call: run_shell (npm test) -> failed (timeout) - tool_call: edit_file (src/checkout.test.ts) -> pending [SUMMARY] The agent is investigating a flaky checkout test. It read the file, ran the test which timed out, and is now attempting to edit the source code to fix it. ``` **Update (Append Only):** ```text ... [Previous Prompt Context - KV Cache Reused Here] ... [NEW EVENTS] - tool_call: edit_file -> completed - tool_call: run_shell (npm test) -> completed (2 passed, 1 failed) - tool_call: read_text_file (src/checkout.ts) -> completed [UPDATE INSTRUCTIONS] Update the summary based on NEW EVENTS only. Keep the previous context. ``` ### The Loop 1. **Incoming Frame:** Agent sends `session/update`. 2. **Batcher:** Buffer frames for ~200ms (debounce). 3. **Formatter:** Convert JSON frames to concise text (don't send raw JSON to LLM, it's too noisy). * *Bad:* `{"method": "tool_call", "status": "completed"}` * *Good:* `[completed] run_shell (npm test)` 4. **LLM Worker:** Push formatted text to `llama.cpp` with the running prompt. 5. **Render:** Update the UI summary instantly. ### The "Killer Feature": Anomaly Detection Because the summary is instant, you can trigger alerts based on the LLM's output: * **"Loop Detected":** The LLM notices the agent is editing/running/testing the same file 3 times. * **"Permission Denied":** The LLM sees a `session/request_permission` with outcome `reject`. * **"Silent Failure":** The summary hasn't changed in 60 seconds but the agent is still connected. ### Hardware Requirements To make this "instant": * **Model:** Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-Q4_K_M (~4GB VRAM). * **Context Window:** Large enough to hold the session history (e.g., 8k or 16k tokens). * **Backend:** `llama.cpp` server (`server -m model.gguf --ctx-size 16384`) or Ollama. This turns your viewer from a passive log into an **active diagnostic copilot**.
